ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried chicken class (FOODON_00002490); no specific Cajun-spiced fried chicken entry exists. FOODON_00002490 used as closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried chicken meat generically; Cajun seasoning and fries accompaniment are not separately coded. Used as closest defensible FoodEx2 parent anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for Cajun fried chicken identified in the clinical terminology hierarchy; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: Half chicken with a side of fries
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is half a Cajun fried chicken with a side of french fries, weighing approximately 550 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (SR Legacy ID 05062) and Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, oven-heated (SR Legacy ID 11408); composite estimate weighted 70% chicken, 30% fries
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry for Cajun fried chicken with fries as a composite dish. Nutrient values derived from a weighted composite: 70% batter-fried chicken (USDA SR Legacy 05062) and 30% oven-heated frozen french fries (USDA SR Legacy 11408), then cross-referenced against the GrabFood-reported per-100g values (energy 218 kcal, protein 13.6 g, fat 15.5 g, carbohydrate 7.3 g) which fall within the plausible range for this composite. Input values from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the primary per-100g figures given their internal consistency; USDA SR Legacy composite serves as the corroborating approved-source anchor. Cajun spice blend contributes negligible macronutrients but elevates sodium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on fried chicken with coating (GI ~60–70) and french fries (GI ~63, University of Sydney GI Database entry)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Cajun fried chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) batter-fried chicken GI approximately 60–70 from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); (2) french fries GI 63 (University of Sydney GI Database). Composite dish GI weighted 70% chicken component and 30% fries component yields approximately 64–65. Glycemic load calculated as: GI 65 × available carbohydrate per serving (7.27 g per 100 g × 550 g serving / 100 = 39.99 g) / 100 = 26.0 GL per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg/100 g; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request reduced Cajun seasoning salt and avoid all additional dipping sauces to keep the single-meal sodium contribution below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is moderate at 7.3 g/100 g (40 g per 550 g serving) with a medium GI of 65, yielding a glycemic load of 26 per serving; the high fat content slows gastric emptying but adds significant caloric density.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume a half-portion (approximately 275 g) occasionally, pairing it with non-starchy vegetables and avoiding high-GI dipping sauces such as ketchup or sweet chilli sauce to limit the postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates trans fats (0.09 g/100 g) and advanced glycation end products (AGEs); saturated fat is 3.64 g/100 g (20 g per 550 g serving), and sodium is 327 mg/100 g; together these elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish; if eaten, limit to a quarter-portion, choose grilled Cajun chicken instead of deep-fried, and avoid adding salt or high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that directly exacerbate joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations; request grilled or baked Cajun chicken to eliminate AGE formation, and monitor tolerance to nightshade-derived spices (cayenne, paprika) in the Cajun blend.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cajun Fried Chicken is a deep-fried chicken dish coated in a Cajun spice blend — typically comprising paprika, cayenne pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, thyme, and black pepper — producing a boldly seasoned, crispy exterior with juicy, tender meat inside. As served in Singapore food-delivery contexts, a standard portion is half a chicken (approximately 385 g chicken) accompanied by a side of french fries (approximately 165 g), totalling around 550 g per serving. The dish is deep-fried in oil, giving it a characteristic golden-brown crust with a crunchy texture and a spicy, smoky flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 218 kcal, 13.6 g protein, 15.5 g fat (3.6 g saturated), 7.3 g carbohydrate, 0.4 g sugar, 0.9 g dietary fibre, and 327 mg sodium. The high sodium content from Cajun seasoning and added salt makes this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and small amounts of trans fats, raising cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 26 per full serving, making portion control important for people with type 2 diabetes. Despite its American-Cajun culinary roots, this dish is widely available across Singapore hawker-adjacent and fast-casual dining platforms and is considered a popular Western-style comfort food in the local context.

Can diabetics eat Cajun fried chicken?
Is Cajun fried chicken high in sodium?
What is the GI of fried chicken?
Is Cajun fried chicken suitable for hypertension?
Cajun fried chicken nutrition facts Singapore

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values originate from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Values are internally consistent and fall within the plausible range corroborated by a USDA SR Legacy composite estimate (70% batter-fried chicken + 30% french fries). No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry for Cajun fried chicken with fries exists. Medium confidence reflects the adapted/estimated source match and the ai_generated provenance of the input.
notes: 1. Per-100g nutrient values are sourced from the GrabFood input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and corroborated against a USDA SR Legacy composite (70% batter-fried chicken SR Legacy 05062 + 30% french fries SR Legacy 11408); no exact approved-source entry for this composite dish exists. USDA FoodData Central is the approved nutrient source anchor used for this record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Cajun fried chicken with fries composite are not individually reported in the GrabFood input and are not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. 3. Cultural classification in the input listed 'Indian' as primary cuisine, which is inconsistent with Cajun fried chicken's American-Cajun origin; corrected to 'American-Cajun (Singapore adaptation)' with region_of_origin set to Singapore to reflect the local food-delivery context. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on batter-fried chicken and french fries entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for Cajun fried chicken exists in any approved GI source. 5. Halal status marked true as chicken is the primary protein and no pork-derived ingredients are listed; however, halal certification of the specific restaurant/vendor has not been independently verified. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.09 g/100 g as reported in the input; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with deep-frying in partially hydrogenated or repeatedly heated oils. 7. Ontology match_type set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact Cajun fried chicken entry exists in either ontology; codes represent the closest defensible parent class.
